poetry.title: Life Gave me a Lemon 🍋
poetry.content:
One Monday morning, when I awoke on my bed,
A lemon fell suddenly on my head.
A strange look this lemon had, not of the usual kind,
It had an ethereal beauty to it, incomprehensible by my mind.


I quickly got up and grabbed the lemon from where it fell,
My nostrils got flooded by an aroma, an otherworldly smell.
I saw a pool from where I took it, it must have bled from the drop,
But inspecting it closer, it was cleaner than a well-polished countertop.


My curiosity piqued, and tasted a fingertips worth;
By God! No such flavor given me this much mirth.
Instinct took over me at that very instant, I must have more!
So hastily I went to the other side of my bedroom door.


In the kitchen, I grabbed the sharpest thing that's in store,
Everything that I needed to get to its delicious core.
But my first attempt was feeble, for I did not penetrate its skin,
Not even the tiniest scratch, not a loss on its glistening sheen.


The second attempt was worse, for it made the lemon jump and roll,
Out of the kitchen, and into the hall.
So to the hall I went, and eagerly chased after it,
But the lemon was faster, out of my sight in a split.


Until I no longer had the idea of where it could possibly be,
I searched every inch of my house, everywhere that I can see.
I know it's in here with me, it must have rolled into a nook—
A nook that will be impossible to look.


After hours of my perilous search, I have decided to retreat,
For I have run out of hope, and finally conceded defeat.
But that lemon will never ever leave my head;
Its mystical aura will be with me until I'm dead.


Oh, to live with such a burden is torturing me every day,
I wish to see you one last time lemon, give me a chance to say:
That I am sorry for taking your comfort from you, I was overcome with emotion,
I hope that you forgive me and accept my desperate devotion.

poetry.title: Grey Sweater
poetry.content:
Girl in gray sweater
Slouching in the bright red chair
chatting with her friend

poetry.title: Slowly
poetry.content:
Time ticking slowly
Slower than a lazy snail
it's agonizing

poetry.content:

It is written on the sign
Yet they still do not

Lay on the massive pillows

Pink shirt guy enters
Holding a box of donuts
I crave those donuts

Left an indent of his shape
Possibly his sweat

poetry.content:
Darker than normal
Maybe since some lights are off
I do wonder why

poetry.title: CCTV
poetry.content:
In the far corner
Sits a lonely camera
It watches us all

poetry.title: GAIC
poetry.content:
They wear blue or white
They come here everyday
To gather our waste

poetry.title: The Haunting👻
poetry.content:
Once upon a dreary midnight. When the sky was void of every starlight,
Stood a half-kept manor where a young man slept.
Deep in his slumber, the young man was, dreaming of glamour,
When all of sudden, he was awoken by unwanted clamor.


"Who's there?!" The young man asked with fright.
But the only answer, was the quietness of the night.
He thought little of it, and tucked himself in.
Little does he know, that's when the hauntings begin.


It started with 2 ghouls, looming like dread from afar, and slowly get closer with every setting of our star.
Closer and closer the apparitions got, the more loud and painful became its chants of rot,
"You cannot overcome us!", "We shall be your ruin!", they said in a synchronous voice.
Slowly and surely, they took the young man's poise.


Next was a phantom, so vulnerable, so deep in its woe.
It made the young man question if it is a friend or a foe.
Friend or Foe? Who knows, for it is easily swayed by the wind.
On certain nights he's a companion, on other nights he's a fiend.


Then there was the ghost of a lovely woman in white.
Who only shows herself in his dreams every night.
Unlike the others, she does not break the young man with fright,
But taunts him with what he longs for, she tortures him with his sight


This went on for weeks then months, then years.
They tried to drive him mad, slowly draining him of his tears.
But the young man stood strong, He overcame his fears,
And gradually, he learned to live with his new peers.

poetry.title: The Box 📦
poetry.content:
I woke up to find a box on the sidewalk, a few feet from my door.
It had no such labels. No sender and receiver address,
only the words "Your burden will be no more.".


What could that mean? I thought while staring at the box.
It looked like it was in amazing condition.
No dents, no tears - so perfect I swore it had a glow,
It only added to my confusion.


I imagined the different items that could fit inside.
"Your burden will be no more." I keep muttering to myself as I thought,
I exhausted everything my mind could provide.


A million dollars or a bomb, A golden ticket or poison.
That was the conclusion that I came to.
Thinking of the worst made me deathly afraid to even lay a finger on the it,
I don't want to move it from its position.


Suffering or Happiness, whatever that box is offering as a solution.
I hurriedly went inside my home and locked the door behind me.
Making sure to not be affected by the box's intention.


As I never left my house for the rest of the day,
So did the thought of the box inside my head.
What if I'm missing on something big? What if I end up dead?
Questions begging to be answered, lingering on my mind as I lay.


Maybe I'm just overthinking it, I thought, my courage starting to grow.
I got up from my bed and opened the door. What's inside the box!? You might be wondering.
I guess we'll never know.


For when I stepped outside, the box was no longer there.
I wondered where it went, when I heard my neighbor.
Bursting with joy. "Thank Jove for this box!" I heard them say.
"We are set for life!", those words echo in my mind every single day.


I wish I had enough courage
I wish I just had enough care.
But it's all too late now - all I could do is just stare.

development.content:
A collaborative gacha-style mobile game designed to teach automotive specifications.

Team: Ric Bryan Masagca, Paul Jazzmir Entena, Kurt Tracy Amador, Johann Lijauco.
